    "R1.1",

  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "When you say “Make this stronger,” which focus should we take? A) Tighten language and punch up phrasing only (sentence-level polish; keep structure/claims unchanged); B) Reframe the abstract’s thesis/contribution and assert novelty more forcefully (may reorganize and adjust claims); C) Make it more conservative and evidence-aligned (reduce/qualify “first” claims, emphasize measured support). Choose A, B, or C.",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "Strengthening may require sharpening the thesis, gap, and contribution framing beyond sentence-level edits.",
    "R1.2": "Ensuring the argument from problem to method to evidence to claims is logically tight is central to making the piece feel stronger.",
    "R1.3": "Claims like superiority over softmax and usefulness for selective prediction must be tightly linked to the reported AUROC and accuracy results.",
    "R1.4": "The text makes multiple “first” claims, so assessing and calibrating asserted novelty relative to nearby ideas is central.",
    "R1.6": "Stronger delivery typically hinges on clearer, more concise, and more forceful sentence-level writing.",
    "RX.1": "We should disclose uncertainty where evidence or novelty assertions are not definitive.",
    "RX.2": "Avoiding unsupported superlatives and overbroad implications is crucial while strengthening claims.",
    "RX.3": "We must limit changes to strengthening the provided text without introducing new experiments or external facts.",
    "RX.4": "Overstating trustworthiness in safety-relevant contexts could mislead, so risk-aware phrasing is needed.",
    "RX.5": "All strengthened statements should remain traceable to the provided artifact’s content and numbers."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: When you say “Make this stronger,” which focus should we take? A) Tighten language and punch up phrasing only (sentence-level polish; keep structure/claims unchanged); B) Reframe the abstract’s thesis/contribution and assert novelty more forcefully (may reorganize and adjust claims); C) Make it more conservative and evidence-aligned (reduce/qualify “first” claims, emphasize measured support). Choose A, B, or C.

rationale.R1.1: Strengthening may require sharpening the thesis, gap, and contribution framing beyond sentence-level edits.
rationale.R1.2: Ensuring the argument from problem to method to evidence to claims is logically tight is central to making the piece feel stronger.
rationale.R1.3: Claims like superiority over softmax and usefulness for selective prediction must be tightly linked to the reported AUROC and accuracy results.
rationale.R1.4: The text makes multiple “first” claims, so assessing and calibrating asserted novelty relative to nearby ideas is central.
rationale.R1.6: Stronger delivery typically hinges on clearer, more concise, and more forceful sentence-level writing.
rationale.RX.1: We should disclose uncertainty where evidence or novelty assertions are not definitive.
rationale.RX.2: Avoiding unsupported superlatives and overbroad implications is crucial while strengthening claims.
rationale.RX.3: We must limit changes to strengthening the provided text without introducing new experiments or external facts.
rationale.RX.4: Overstating trustworthiness in safety-relevant contexts could mislead, so risk-aware phrasing is needed.
rationale.RX.5: All strengthened statements should remain traceable to the provided artifact’s content and numbers.
